blocks.text: Jobs can be registered inline with hound.on() or defined as reusable JobDefinition objects using defineJob(). The defineJob() approach colocates the handler with its data shape, queue config, and retry options — and provides full TypeScript inference across your app.
Call defineJob() with an event name and a handler. The second generic parameter types the ctx.data payload:
Pass the definition to hound.on() to register it:
Both hound.on() and defineJob() accept the same options object:

rows: Max simultaneous executions for this handler
Debounce window in ms — collapses repeated emits
Cron expression to self-schedule the job
Cron-only. If false (default), missed ticks are skipped on restart / Reaper recovery; if true, the missed tick is backfilled. Throws if set without repeat.pattern.

blocks.text: Instead of manually importing and calling hound.on() for every job, set jobDirs in HoundOptions and Hound will scan those directories on start() and register every *.job.ts file it finds.
File naming convention

Any file whose name ends in .job.ts will be picked up. The event name is taken from the first argument of defineJob() inside the file — not from the filename. A common convention is to mirror the event name in the filename for clarity:
Directory conventions

There is no enforced structure — any directory name works. A useful pattern is to separate one-off tasks from recurring cron jobs into their own folders, for example _tasks/ and _scheduled/. The underscore prefix is a convention to signal that these folders contain infrastructure files rather than application modules.
What gets registered

Hound imports each *.job.ts file and looks for any exported value that was created with defineJob(). Both named exports and default exports are supported. You can export multiple jobs from a single file — all of them will be registered.
jobDirs is also used by deno task codegen to generate the HoundJobMap type, giving you full type-safety on hound.emit() without any manual type wiring.

Codegen reads the same jobDirs you pass to Hound.create() and produces two files: hound-types.ts (the HoundJobMap interface) and hound-client.ts (a typed HTTP client). Once generated, every hound.emit() call is fully type-checked — wrong event names or mismatched payloads are caught at compile time.
Setup
Create a dedicated plugin file and add a deno task to run it:
What gets generated
Running deno task codegen produces two files. The first is HoundJobMap — a typed interface that maps every event name to its payload shape, derived from your defineJob exports:
The second file is HoundClient — a portable HTTP client wired to HoundJobMap. It can be used from any runtime (Node, Bun, browser) to emit jobs through the HTTP gateway:
Using the generated client
Why run codegen

items: Catch typos in event names at compile time, not at runtime
Payload types are inferred directly from your defineJob<App, Payload> generics — no duplication
The generated client works anywhere: other Deno services, Node backends, or browser frontends
Re-run after adding or renaming any *.job.ts file to keep types in sync

blocks.text: The generated files are derived from source — commit them but do not edit them by hand. Add // @generated or similar if your linter would otherwise flag them.
